item.title: Environmental News in Brief: Summer 2026 - Earth Island Institute
item.description:
FINDINGS

Sea level rise is among the most pressing climate challenges, and scientists have long warned of the real risk it poses to coastal communities. Now they say that risk is even bigger than they thought because, it turns out, ocean waters are actually higher than they thought.

According to a new study published in Nature in March, baseline sea levels are nearly a foot higher, on average, than most research has accounted for. That means as many as 132 million more people than previously estimated may be at risk if sea levels rise by three feet by the end of the century.

The discrepancy traces to a methodological error. Researchers have traditionally estimated current sea levels using a so-called geoid model that factors in information about gravity and Earth’s rotation. This approach, however, does not account for conditions on the ground, including winds, tides, and currents, which can push waters significantly higher. As a result, in some places — like Southeast Asia and the Indo-Pacific — scientists have been underestimating current sea levels by more than three feet. In other places, the difference is more modest.

The findings could have significant implications for communities around the world. “Simply put, if sea level in reality is higher for your particular island or coastal city than was previously assumed, the impacts from sea level rise will happen sooner than projected,” Philip Minderhoud, one of the study authors and an associate professor at Wageningen University & Research in the Netherlands, told CNN.

The researchers based their findings on 385 peer-reviewed studies and found that around 90 percent failed to factor in on-the-ground conditions. Even among the 10 percent that did, the vast majority failed to integrate this information correctly.

The silver lining, if there is one? Even before the study was published, researchers had begun doing a better job of estimating current sea levels. Now that the research error has been identified, that trend will likely continue.

FUTURE TENSE

Small, portable solar panels that help tenants and homeowners reduce their reliance on fossil fuels seem like a no-brainer. They offer a win-win for both people and the planet, reducing utility costs alongside greenhouse gas emissions. The only problem? American utility companies don’t like these “balcony solar” units and restrict their use across most of the US.

Thankfully, that may be changing — and Virginia is leading the way. In March, the Virginia Senate passed a measure exempting plug-in solar devices from complicated hook-up requirements, and preventing utilities from restricting their use. In May, Governor Abigail Spanberger signed the legislation, which was previously passed by the House of Delegates. The move makes Virginia the second state, after Utah, to legalize balcony solar.

This is “yet another small step toward a broader transition in Virginia’s energy economy, from its historic reliance on coal toward solar and battery storage,” Tony Smith, president and founder of Secure Solar Futures, told UtilityDive.

Utilities, which currently generate only about 20 percent of their energy from solar and wind, are pushing back, of course, citing safety concerns. But solar advocates say it’s about their bottom line; balcony solar has a stellar safety record.

With utility costs and emissions rising, here’s to hoping 48 more states will follow Virginia and Utah’s lead.

POLICYWATCH

It’s no secret that President Trump hates wind power. He’s called the renewable energy everything from unreliable to “the scam of the century.” He has pulled federal funding from at least a dozen offshore wind projects, and now his administration is paying companies to not develop wind leases.

In late March, the US Department of the Interior announced that it would refund nearly $1 billion to the French multinational company Total Energies to abandon its wind-farm leases in the Atlantic Ocean. In late April, the Trump administration offered another billion-dollar buyout, this time divided between two companies — Bluepoint Wind and Golden State Wind — with projects on opposite coasts. All these deals hinge on redirecting the “reimbursement” money toward oil and gas, particularly into offshore oil projects along the Gulf coast.

“This is a backdoor deal done with zero transparency, no public process, and no consideration of the impacts to ratepayers in states that had been planning on that offshore wind to meet their energy needs,” Elizabeth Klein, who formerly led the Interior Department’s Bureau of Ocean Energy Management, told Grist.

Perhaps it’s no surprise that just a week after the Total Energies news, a federal committee known as the “God Squad” exempted Gulf-based oil-and-gas activities from the Endangered Species Act. The decision was prompted by a request from Pete Hegseth, US secretary of defense, who invoked the need to ramp up domestic oil production due to “national security” concerns.

The Gulf of Mexico is home to at least 20 threatened or endangered species, including the green sea turtle, the giant manta ray, mountainous star coral, and the critically endangered Rice’s whale. Several Gulf and environmental groups have sued the Trump administration over this decision. A hearing on the case is pending.

UPDATE

In late February, nearly a decade after the Standing Rock protests, a North Dakota judge finalized a mind-boggling guilty verdict against Greenpeace for its role in the Indigenous-led uprising.

The verdict traces back to the 2016-2017 protests, led by Indigenous activists, that aimed to stop construction of the Dakota Access Pipeline and draw attention to the threat it posed to local waterways. Though the effort ultimately failed — the pipeline was completed in 2017 — in 2019 Energy Transfer, the company behind the pipeline, sued Greenpeace in a North Dakota court. It alleged, with little evidence, that the green group defamed the company and orchestrated criminal behavior at the protests.

Following a highly watched 2025 trial, a jury found Greenpeace guilty, ordering the nonprofit to pay Energy Transfer more than $660 million in damages for its role in the protests. The damages were ultimately reduced to a (still whopping) $345 million.

Greenpeace denies the claims made against it and describes the suit as another Big Oil strategy to shut down opposition to its climate-damaging activities. It calls the case an attack on First Amendment rights and a clear example of a strategic lawsuit against public participation, or SLAPP. Greenpeace plans to appeal the verdict. It also countersued Energy Transfer in the Netherlands last year under a European Union law aimed at curbing SLAPP suits. That case is still ongoing.

“Speaking out against corporations that cause environmental harm should never be deemed unlawful,” Marco Simons, interim general counsel at Greenpeace USA and Greenpeace Fund, said in a statement. “It is guaranteed by the US Constitution, and is essential to the protection of communities and the health of democracy.”

“We will not be silenced,” Greenpeace International Executive Director Mads Christensen added.

“We will only get louder, joining our voices to those of our allies all around the world against the corporate polluters and billionaire oligarchs who prioritize profits over people and the planet.

CALL OF THE WILD

For decades, scientists have known the long-fingered possum (Tous ayamaruensis) and the ring-tailed glider (Dactylonax kambuayai) only through Australian fossils dating back to the last ice age. The two marsupial species were believed to have gone extinct nearly 6,000 years ago. Except, they didn’t.

Much to the delighted surprise of the scientific community, both species — a striped marsupial named after an abnormally long digit on each hand, and a relative of Australian greater gliders that gets around by swinging from branch to branch using its tail — have been photographed alive.

Researchers at the Australian Museum, who recently published a report about their once-in-a-lifetime discovery in Records of the Australian Museum, found the species still living in the remote rainforests of the Vogelkop Peninsula in Papuan Indonesia.

“The likelihood of finding mammals belonging to the ‘Lazarus species’ [species that appear to come back from the dead] is “almost zero,” Tim Flannery, a zoologist and lead author of the paper, told The Guardian. “It’s sort of a crowning glory in my career as a biologist.”

The researchers achieved this thanks to local help: After years of collecting evidence of the species’ existence — including photographs taken by local researchers, fossil fragments, and misidentified museum specimens — they worked with elders from the Indigenous Tambrauw and Maybrat communities who helped track them down.

The exact location of these animals is being concealed in order to save them from wildlife traffickers.

UPWELL

The main ocean current system in the Atlantic — the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC), which brings warm water northward toward the Arctic and carries cold water southward — is one of the most powerful distributors of heat around the globe. The AMOC is the reason that northern Europe is a couple of degrees warmer than it ought to be given its latitude. But that could change very soon.

Scientists have discovered that the current is closer to collapse than previously thought.

The AMOC is already known to be at its weakest point in 1,600 years, as a result of human-caused planetary warming. But researchers weren’t able to forecast the time of the collapse due to the complexity of the AMOC system and uncertainty over just how much the world would warm in the future. The system, they said, could break down either within a decade or two, or several centuries from now.

A new analysis — which combines real-world ocean observations with climate models to reduce uncertainty — has found that the models predicting the biggest slowdown of the current system are the most realistic. The finding, published in Science Advances in April, estimates that the AMOC will slow down by 42 to 58 percent by 2100, a level that could lead to its collapse.

The system’s collapse would shift the tropical rainfall belt (which millions of people rely on to grow their food), lead to extreme cold winters and summer droughts in western Europe, and increase sea level rise around the Atlantic by another 50 to 100 centimeters.

Despite this analysis, climate scientists are still arguing over exactly when the AMOC will collapse. But there’s one thing they agree on: Even without a collapse, a weakening of the AMOC could have serious climate impacts.

CALL OF THE WILD

It’s been two decades since the release of Happy Feet, the animated musical film that swept the world up in a fawning frenzy over emperor penguins. Now, the adorable flightless birds are in the spotlight again, this time as a sobering reminder that their days on the planet might be numbered.

The emperor penguin (Aptenodytes forsteri), alongside the Antarctic fur seal (Arctocephalus gazella), has officially been classified as endangered on the International Union for Conservation of Nature’s Red List of Threatened Species. Researchers estimate that global warming-induced declines in sea ice will nearly halve the penguin’s population by the 2080s. Increasing food scarcity has already caused the seal’s population to plummet by half.

While this is devastating news, there were warning signs. When it came to emperor penguins, in 2022, researchers found that the seabirds were dying due to a lack of sea ice in western Antarctica’s Bellingshausen Sea. And as Mongabay reports, two years prior to that, scientists discovered that a colony farther north had failed to reproduce for three consecutive years.

With respect to Antarctic fur seals, their populations had rebounded after a 1972 hunting ban. But over the past 12 years, their numbers have dropped drastically again — from an estimated 2 million adults to fewer than 1 million, largely due to a decline in the availability of krill in their warming habitat. (Krill are moving to deeper, colder waters and farther offshore.)

Krill distribution shifts are “unlikely to be reversible” unless the world curtails greenhouse gases soon, Jaume Forcada, a marine mammal scientist at the British Antarctic Survey who contributed to the updated assessment, told The New York Times.

The species’ fates aren’t sealed, but the chance of saving them is slim.

TOXICS

In what’s being called a public-health win by many, agri-tech company Syngenta has announced that it will cease the global production of paraquat, an herbicide linked to Parkinson’s disease, by June 2026. An effective killer of broadleaf weeds and grasses, paraquat is currently banned in 70 countries but remains in wide use in US agriculture, with over 10 million pounds applied to fields annually.

While Syngenta says its move is motivated purely by commercial reasons — mainly due to “significant competition” from generic producers of paraquat — the early March announcement came on the heels of thousands of lawsuits against the company by US citizens who allege they developed Parkinson’s disease after being exposed to the pesticide.

The company manufactures and distributes the weed killer from a facility in St. Gabriel, Louisiana. The city is part of a region nicknamed “cancer alley” due to high cancer risk linked to industrial air pollution.

Evidence of paraquat’s toxicity has long been established. A 2017 memo by the Environmental Protection Agency noted that ingesting paraquat can be fatal, and even minimal contact can result in injury to skin, eyes, or lungs. What’s more, The Guardian and The New Lede recently obtained internal company files that prove that Syngenta knew of paraquat’s association with Parkinson’s disease decades ago, and nevertheless tried to obscure those connections.

“For decades we have warned that certain pesticides increase the risk of Parkinson’s and other serious diseases. This moment proves that advocacy, data, and courage can change the trajectory of disease,” Michael Okun, director of the University of Florida’s Fixel Institute, who has been advocating for a paraquat ban, told The Guardian.

Though Syngenta is ending production, other manufacturers of the chemical will continue supplying it.

FOREST & THE TREES

The US Forest Service may be best known for managing the nation’s wealth of forests. But it also plays another critical role as the largest forest research organization in the world, investigating climate change, wildfire, soil health, drought, timber production, ecosystem health, and much more.

That role as a global research leader is now being diminished. In April, the Trump administration announced that it would be closing 57 of the Forest Service’s 77 research stations across 31 states.

“There are research plots and experimental forests that have been diligently studied for decades,” Kevin Hood, executive director of Forest Service Employees for Environmental Ethics, told Stateline. “This could be a loss of a lot of knowledge.”

The administration says it will consolidate the research into a single facility in Colorado. At the same time, it is moving the agency’s headquarters from Washington, DC, to Utah. Other facilities are still under evaluation, indicating that further closures may occur.

The research cuts could be a blow to diverse constituencies, from environmental advocates to forest-adjacent communities.

As Robert Bonnie, the under secretary of agriculture for natural resources and environment during the Obama administration, put it, “Nobody was asking for this.”

AROUND THE WORLD

In March — the most abnormally hot month in 132 years of US record-keeping — waters in the tropical Pacific Ocean were turning steamy. This prompted weather agencies around the world to start issuing alerts for an incoming El Niño. By fall in the Northern Hemisphere, meteorologists warned, we might see a “super El Niño,” triggered by sea surface temperatures soaring well above average. There is “real potential for the strongest El Niño event in 140 years,” Paul Roundy, an El Niño expert at the University at Albany, SUNY, told Yale Climate Connections in early April.

You’ve surely heard of El Niño, but you’d be forgiven if your knowledge stems mainly from a 1997 Saturday Night Live sketch where, dressed like a Mexican wrestler, the late Chris Farley roared, “El Niño is Spanish for… the niño. Any of you hurricanes who are listening, step on up, because nobody can kick El Niño.”

There’s some truth to that threat. The ocean-atmospheric phenomenon known as the El Niño Southern Oscillation (ENSO) is the planet’s single largest weather-shaper. Unlike its cooler, tamer sister, La Niña, El Niño is the warmer phase of ENSO, and it’s associated with brute force — from heavy rainfall and flooding in the southern United States, parts of South America, and central Asia to intense heat and drought in India, southern Africa, and Australia. Scientists have theorized that El Niño played a role in crop failures that helped spark the French Revolution, as well as in the downfall of several ancient civilizations.

El Niños have been getting warmer since the 1950s. Scientists are warning that this next one could shatter temperature records in 2026 and 2027. The domino effects of warmer seawater and increased rain, drought, and heat could be devastating for fisheries, forests, crops, wildlife, and, of course, humans. While nothing in weather prediction is certain, here are five countries that should brace for El Niño.

SOURCES: AccuWeather, Associated Press, Australian Bureau of Meteorology, Climate Council, CNN, NASA, National Geographic, NOAA, Reuters, Saturday Night Live, Science, Scripps Institute of Oceanography, Times of India, Veritas News, We Are Water Foundation, Weather and Climate Extremes, Yale Climate Connections

1 PERU

Spanish colonists are thought to have first noticed this warm-water phenomenon off the coast of Peru in the 1500s, and later named it El Niño, which also means “Christ Child” when capitalized. (In Peru, El Niño often arrives around Christmas.) The country initially sees unusually warm ocean temperatures along with reduced upwelling of cold seawater and nutrients to the surface. This can have severe consequences for marine species and local fisheries, including the harvest of anchovy, sardine, mackerel, shrimp, tuna, and hake.

Early in the year, El Niño means rain. And even in pre-El Niño March this year, Peruvian fruit growers had already lost some 6,000 hectares of crops due to heavy storms and excess water. According to the Lima Chamber of Commerce, even a weak or moderate El Niño could bring daily losses of over $85 million across the agricultural, trade, and manufacturing sectors in seven Peruvian regions.

2 UNITED STATES

El Niño often speeds up winds in the upper atmosphere of the central Atlantic, weakening hurricane season, which peaks in August and September. However, toasty ocean temperatures help the hurricanes that do form become more destructive. In the Pacific, warm seawater fuels consistent moisture and winds that brew into fall storms. By winter, parades of these storms slam Southern California and several southern states, triggering floods and landslides.

California sees higher sea levels and flooding during this weather cycle, too, since warmer temperatures cause thermal expansion; the 2015-2016 super El Niño, for instance, raised the state’s sea levels as much as 11 inches. One silver lining is that parched regions like the Colorado River Basin could get deluged with rain and snow, boosting water supplies.

3 AUSTRALIA

Warming seawater in the central and eastern Pacific Ocean during El Niño years increases rainfall in the Pacific instead of around northern Australia, where it usually falls. This means a higher likelihood of Australian drought.

Nine out of the ten driest winter-spring periods on record for eastern Australia occurred during El Niño years, as did severe droughts in the Murray-Darling Basin in the southeast. However, less cloud cover also creates cooler nighttime temperatures. Southern New South Wales and northern Victoria can see 15 to 30 percent more frost days during El Niño winters and springs.

By contrast, El Niño tends to bring spiking temperatures across southern Australia, particularly during the second half of the year, when bushfire alerts also tick into the red. Off the coast, marine heat waves associated with El Niño have triggered mass bleaching on both the Great Barrier Reef and Ningaloo Reef.

4 INDIA

While El Niño never guarantees drought, a 2006 study of more than 130 years of rainfall data in India revealed that severe droughts did always coincide with this weather pattern. In such cases, the monsoon rains that normally pour from June through September are dialed way down, straining crops such as millet, oilseeds, and cotton. There’s evidence that three great famines of the late nineteenth century were all linked to El Niño.

The India Meteorological Department is forecasting below-normal rainfall in 2026 for the country’s monsoon core, which encompasses regions in western and central India that rely on rain for farming. Drinking water and hydroelectric power are also at risk. Given that much of the country’s surface and groundwater are already overexploited, largely due to unregulated irrigation practices,
another low-rainfall year will present major socioeconomic challenges.

5 CHINA

The final stop on El Niño’s year-long world tour is the region around China and Japan, which will likely get hit with heavy rainfall next spring. Summer floods often follow, like the catastrophic Yangtze River flood that killed 3,000 people and caused $20 billion in damages during the summer following the super El Niño of 1997-1998.

And while the rain is helpful for farming, El Niño also brings high temperatures, which can spark dry spells and drought, and amount to a thinner snowpack, lowering crop resistance to cold. As the world’s largest food producer, China is working to mitigate such climate risks while continuing to boost agricultural output. Researchers have suggested creating El Niño and La Niña crop-sensitivity maps to help with localized disaster management.

FLIP SIDE

In the wild, Eurasian blue tits line their nests with bits of aromatic plants, such as lavender, yarrow, and apple mint, that contain volatile compounds, which help repel blood-sucking parasites. In cities, they are picking something else entirely — discarded cigarette butts.

Researchers in Poland, who delved into growing reports of urban blue tits bringing cigarette butts into their nests, or building nests in ashtrays and butt bins, say that the birds might be using the material as “an opportunistic adaptive strategy, mimicking the function of aromatic plants” to help keep parasites in check. Tobacco, they note, “is often cited as an efficient insect repellent and insecticide.”

To test if the butts actually acted as a chemical shield from bugs, they studied 33 blue tit nests in urban parks and forests around the city of Łódź, in central Poland. In 11 of those, they added two smoked Marlboro cigarette butts five days after the eggs hatched. In 11 others, they replaced the nests with sterilized nests fashioned from moss and cotton, and in the last 11, they did nothing except add two ends from unsmoked cigarettes.

After 13 days, the researchers found that chicks in nests with smoked cigarette butts and in the artificial sterile nests were healthier and had less parasite-related blood loss than the nests with unsmoked butts. The findings, published in the journal Animal Behavior in March, show that smoked cigarette butts were, in some ways, linked with healthier chicks.

While this is potentially good news, the scientists cited a serious concern: These butts also contain toxic chemicals like arsenic and heavy metals that could affect bird health later in life. Past research in Mexico on urban house finches, who also use cigarette butts in their nests, showed that while the chicks appeared healthier, their blood cells bore signs of genetic damage.

“In a world increasingly dominated by urban landscapes and human waste, understanding how wildlife responds to and potentially exploits these changes is a crucial step toward predicting and managing species resilience,” the researchers write.

FINDINGS

Resistance comes in all forms, including 800-page reports. That became clear in early March when scientists released a long-anticipated paper digging into the current state of nature in the US, a study that had been killed by the Trump administration.

The first-of-its-kind report on ecosystem health across the country was commissioned by the Biden administration back in 2022. Federal scientists were weeks away from releasing their first draft when Trump assumed his second term in office in January 2025. He used an executive order to cancel the project. But the scientists shepherding the effort continued the work on their own, with help from private funders, and have now released their preliminary assessment in an 868-page first draft.

The report highlights significant declines in ecosystem health and biodiversity across the nation, and it includes analysis about how those declines harm human communities. It features environmental justice, an issue that has been suppressed by the Trump administration, alongside solutions and examples of resilience.

“The future is not fixed,” Phillip Levin, who led the assessment, told The New York Times. “Conservation, restoration, and renewed connections between people and nature can improve ecosystem health and strengthen community resilience.”

The report was open for public comment through the end of May, and final publication is expected in the fall.

Hats off to the revolutionary scientists who made it happen.

We don’t have a paywall because, as a nonprofit publication, our mission is to inform, educate and inspire action to protect our living world. Which is why we rely on readers like you for support. If you believe in the work we do, please consider making a tax-deductible year-end donation to our Green Journalism Fund.

Get four issues of the magazine at the discounted rate of $20.

item.title: Scholar leads new global project on climate adaptation - Gates Cambridge
item.description:
Victoria Herrmann is head of an organisation leading Heritage Adapts!, a new global campaign to unite the heritage sector on plans for locally-led climate adaptation.

Gates Cambridge Scholar Victoria Herrmann is one of the driving forces behind Heritage Adapts!, the first global campaign uniting the heritage sector behind a shared mission: to bring together at least 3,000 sites and practices taking locally-led climate adaptation action by 2030.

Victoria is Director of Preserving Legacies: A Future for Our Past, a National Geographic project that empowers communities worldwide with the scientific knowledge and technical training to implement climate adaptation plans to safeguard their cultural heritage.

The Heritage Adapts! campaign is spearheaded by a coalition led by Preserving Legacies. It launched last week and mobilises local stewards behind a collective pledge, equipping them with the technical guidance, localised data access and global connections they need to succeed. Recognised under the UN’s Global Climate Action Agenda as one of 120 promising climate delivery plans, Heritage Adapts! is about strengthening community resilience through protecting heritage.

The launch of Heritage Adapts! comes as climate risks to heritage continue to grow. Climate-related hazards across UNESCO-designated sites have increased 40% in a decade, and more than one in four could hit potentially irreversible tipping points by 2050, according to a recent UNESCO report. But Heritage Adapts! says this data covers only a fraction of the world’s heritage and that most sites and practices that communities value remain untracked by international frameworks.

In a press release about the launch, the organisation says: “That gap is itself part of the problem. Heritage adaptation also still remains largely absent from climate finance and policy, leaving many stewards with limited funding and support. Yet heritage is not just at risk – it is part of the solution, a source of community resilience and time-tested solutions relevant today.”

Heritage Adapts!, with its founding coalition of more than 100 organisations and stewards globally, aims to fill that gap and accelerate climate adaptation for all types of heritage.

Andrew Potts, Policy Director of the Preserving Legacies programme, says: “As the world hurtles toward 2 degrees Celsius of warming, we are in a race to help communities – and the culture and heritage that sustain them – adapt while there is still time. The UN’s new Global Goal on adapting cultural heritage can be transformative, but only if it fuels real support for every site and cultural practice. Around the world, partners are uniting to urgently seize this moment. That shared resolve is the driving force behind Heritage Adapts!”

At its core is an online community of action platform built around a self-paced programme through every stage of adaptation, making the process accessible no matter the starting point.

Dan Ioschpe, COP 30 Climate High-Level Champion, says: “This initiative is critical because culture and heritage connect communities to their identity, land, history and future. They shape how people understand risk, respond to change, and build resilience. As emphasised during COP30, climate action begins and ends with people, and culture is one of the most powerful tools for lasting change. This initiative demonstrates the strengths of the climate action agenda and its alignment with the global goal on adaptation as a platform for multi-stakeholder collaboration and implementation.”

Victoria [2014], who is also a National Geographic Explorer, Assistant Research Professor at Georgetown University and Senior Fellow at The Arctic Institute, has spent the past decade leading research initiatives and directing capacity building programmes to support communities adapt on the front lines of the climate crisis and safeguard their cultural heritage.

For more information, go to www.heritageadapts.org.
